title: Training a 65B Model on a Single GPU
concept: By combining 4-bit NF4 weights and LoRA adapters, QLoRA makes it possible to train a 65-billion parameter model on a single consumer GPU card.

exercises.explain: QLoRA stands for Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation, combining quantization (weight compression) with LoRA (adapter-based fine-tuning).

cards.title: AI on a Budget
cards.body: Training a massive 65-billion parameter AI model used to require a whole server room of expensive GPUs. But what if you could do it on just one graphics card inside a normal home computer? Let's find out how!

cards.title: What is QLoRA?
cards.body: QLoRA (Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation) is a smart training method. It shrinks the massive 65-billion parameter model into a highly compressed 4-bit format (called NF4) to save memory, and then trains only a tiny set of extra weights (called LoRA adapters).

cards.title: The Mural Sticker
cards.body: Imagine you want to paint a massive mural (a 65B model), but you only have a tiny bucket of paint. Instead of buying hundreds of expensive new buckets, you paint a small, high-detail sticker (LoRA) and stick it onto a pre-faded background. The background is compressed so tightly it barely takes up space, but the final mural still looks stunning.

cards.title: QLoRA in Action
cards.body: Normally, loading a 65-billion parameter model for training requires multiple enterprise-grade A100 GPUs costing tens of thousands of dollars. With QLoRA, you can fit and train that same 65B model on a single, much cheaper consumer GPU (like a RTX 3090 or 4090) right at home.

cards.title: Does Quality Drop?
cards.body: People think compressing a model to 4-bits makes it lose its intelligence and perform poorly. However, tests show that QLoRA preserves the model's original performance while drastically reducing the hardware cost.

cards.title: Traditional vs. QLoRA
cards.body: Look at how QLoRA compares to standard full training when dealing with a massive 65B model.

left.text: Requires multiple expensive server GPUs because weights are stored in full 16-bit or 32-bit precision.

right.text: Fits on a single consumer GPU by compressing base weights to 4-bit NF4 and training tiny LoRA adapters.

exercises.prompt: How does QLoRA allow a 65B model to be trained on a single GPU?

options.text: It deletes 90% of the model parameters permanently
It speeds up internet connection to cloud servers
It combines 4-bit NF4 compression with trainable LoRA adapters
It physically modifies the GPU chip hardware

exercises.explain: By combining 4-bit NF4 quantization to shrink the base model and LoRA to train small adapter weights, QLoRA lets you train 65B models on a single GPU.

exercises.prompt: True or False: QLoRA makes it possible to train a 65B model on a single GPU.
exercises.explain: True! QLoRA is specifically designed to allow training of massive models (like 65B parameters) on a single GPU by keeping the base model in 4-bit NF4 format.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the statement about QLoRA compression.
exercises.sentence: QLoRA keeps the large model weights compressed in the _ format to save GPU memory.
exercises.explain: QLoRA keeps the main model frozen in a highly compressed 4-bit format called NF4, while training smaller adapters.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes an AI system being trained using QLoRA?

options.text: An AI model is compressed to 4-bit NF4 format, and then learns new chatting styles on a single GPU using small adapters.
A human software engineer writes thousands of rules in Python to teach a bot how to speak.
A simple automation script changes text color based on user inputs.

exercises.explain: The second option describes a human manually coding rules, which is not an AI model learning via QLoRA. The third is a simple script. The first option correctly describes a model adapting and learning with QLoRA.

exercises.prompt: How does QLoRA achieve the feat of training a massive 65-billion parameter model on a single GPU?

options.text: 4-bit NF4 weight compression combined with tiny trainable LoRA adapters
Using standard 16-bit LoRA adapters without compressing the base model weights
Moving all model weights to the computer's CPU and system RAM during training
Splitting the model across 16 expensive server-grade GPUs simultaneously

exercises.explain: QLoRA allows us to train massive 65B models on a single GPU by combining 4-bit NF4 weight quantization to save memory, and LoRA adapters to handle the actual learning updates.
